Go
You said:
import mongoose, { Schema, Document } from "mongoose"; export interface IMachine extends Document { seller: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; machineName: string; category: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; brandModel?: string; yearOfManufacture?: number; condition?: "New" | "Good" | "Used"; machineCapacityHP?: number; fuelType?: "Diesel" | "Petrol"; description?: string; pricing?: { priceType: "Per Hour" | "Per Day" | "Per Acre"; rentalPrice: number; minimumRentalDuration?: number; securityDeposit?: number; }; location?: { address?: string; village?: string; district?: string; state?: string; pincode?: string; type?: "Point"; coordinates?: number[]; }; images?: string[]; operator?: { operatorIncluded: boolean; operatorName?: string; operatorPhone?: string; }; transport?: { transportAvailable: boolean; transportCost?: number; }; isPublished: boolean; wizardStep: number; } const machineSchema = new Schema<IMachine>( { seller: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "User", required: true }, machineName: { type: String, required: true }, category: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "Category", required: true }, brandModel: String, yearOfManufacture: Number, condition: { type: String, enum: ["New", "Good", "Used"] }, machineCapacityHP: Number, fuelType: { type: String, enum: ["Diesel", "Petrol"] }, description: String, pricing: { priceType: { type: String, enum: ["Per Hour", "Per Day", "Per Acre"] }, rentalPrice: Number, minimumRentalDuration: Number, securityDeposit: Number }, location: { address: String, village: String, district: String, state: String, pincode: String, type: { type: String, enum: ["Point"] }, coordinates: { type: [Number], default: undefined } }, images: [String], operator: { operatorIncluded: { type: Boolean, default: false }, operatorName: String, operatorPhone: String }, transport: { transportAvailable: { type: Boolean, default: false }, transportCost: Number }, isPublished: { type: Boolean, default: false }, wizardStep: { type: Number, default: 1 } }, { timestamps: true } ); machineSchema.index({ location: "2dsphere" }); export default mongoose.model<IMachine>("Machine", machineSchema); avoid location only taken as address: String, village: String, district: String, state: String, pincode: String,

ChatGPT said:

If you don’t want GeoJSON coordinates and only want address-based location, you should remove the GeoSpatial structure (type, coordinates, 2dsphere index) from the schema.

Right now your schema is prepared for map / radius search.
But if your requirement is only address fields, simplify it.

✅ Simplified Location Schema (Recommended for your case)

Updated IMachine

TypeScript
location?: {
  address?: string;
  village?: string;
  district?: string;
  state?: string;
  pincode?: string;
};

Updated machineSchema

TypeScript
location: {
  address: String,
  village: String,
  district: String,
  state: String,
  pincode: String
},

❌ Remove These

Delete this part completely:

TypeScript
type?: "Point";
coordinates?: number[];

And also remove this line:

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ location: "2dsphere" });

📦 Final Location Object in DB

Example document in MongoDB:

JSON
location: {
  address: "Near bus stand",
  village: "Kakkanad",
  district: "Ernakulam",
  state: "Kerala",
  pincode: "682030"
}

⚠️ But Think Like a Production Engineer

Two approaches exist:

1️⃣ Address Only (What you are doing)

Pros

  • Simple

  • Easy form

  • Enough for small marketplace

Cons

  • Cannot search machines within 10km

  • Cannot show machines on map

2️⃣ Address + Coordinates (Industry Standard)

Used by:

  • Uber

  • Swiggy

  • OLX

  • Airbnb

Example:

TypeScript
location: {
  address: String,
  village: String,
  district: String,
  state: String,
  pincode: String,

  coordinates: {
    type: [Number] // [lng, lat]
  }
}

Then you can do queries like:

JavaScript
find machines within 5km

⭐ My Professional Advice (For Your Project)

Since you are building a machine rental marketplace, best schema is:

TypeScript
location: {
  address: String,
  village: String,
  district: String,
  state: String,
  pincode: String,

  type: {
    type: String,
    enum: ["Point"],
    default: "Point"
  },

  coordinates: {
    type: [Number]
  }
}

Because later you can build:

  • 🔎 Find machines near farmer

  • 🗺 Map view

  • 🚜 Distance based search

If you want, I can also show you

✔ Professional OLX style machine schema
✔ How to search machines within 10km in MongoDB
✔ How Swiggy / Uber location system works
✔ Best DB structure for your machine rental marketplace

These will upgrade your backend architecture a lot.


You said:
yes

ChatGPT said:

Great 👍 Since you said yes, I’ll show you the professional schema structure used in real marketplace apps (OLX / Uber style) for your Machine Rental Marketplace.

Your current schema is already good, but we can improve scalability and clarity.

🏗 Recommended Production Schema Structure

Instead of mixing everything deeply, structure it clearly.

TypeScript
location: {
  address: String,
  village: String,
  district: String,
  state: String,
  pincode: String,

  geo: {
    type: {
      type: String,
      enum: ["Point"],
      default: "Point"
    },
    coordinates: {
      type: [Number], // [longitude, latitude]
      index: "2dsphere"
    }
  }
}

📦 Example MongoDB Document

JSON
{
  "machineName": "Tractor",
  "category": "67ab23df34",

  "location": {
    "address": "Near bus stand",
    "village": "Kakkanad",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682030",

    "geo": {
      "type": "Point",
      "coordinates": [76.348, 10.015]
    }
  }
}

🚀 Why This Structure is Powerful

1️⃣ Normal address display

Show location easily:

Kakkanad, Ernakulam

2️⃣ Geo search (very powerful)

Example: Find machines within 10km

TypeScript
Machine.find({
  "location.geo": {
    $near: {
      $geometry: {
        type: "Point",
        coordinates: [lng, lat]
      },
      $maxDistance: 10000
    }
  }
})

3️⃣ Future features you can build

With coordinates you can build:

🚜 Machines near farmer

📍 Map view

🚚 Transport distance pricing

⭐ Nearby machines first

This is how Uber / Swiggy / Airbnb / OLX search works.

🧠 Another Professional Improvement (Important)

Add indexes for search performance.

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ category: 1 });

machineSchema.index({
  machineName: "text",
  description: "text"
});

Now users can search:

tractor
harvester
rotavator

⭐ Bonus: Machine Listing Status

Instead of only isPublished, many marketplaces use:

TypeScript
status: {
  type: String,
  enum: ["draft", "pending", "published", "blocked"],
  default: "draft"
}

Example workflow:

draft → seller editing
pending → waiting admin approval
published → live
blocked → admin removed

🧠 If I were building your project professionally

Final structure:

Machine
 ├ seller
 ├ machineName
 ├ category
 ├ machineSpecs
 ├ pricing
 ├ location
 ├ images
 ├ operator
 ├ transport
 ├ status
 ├ wizardStep

Clean and scalable.

🚀 Next Important Thing For Your Project

You are building multi-step machine creation (wizardStep).

Next big backend feature you should implement:

✅ Save machine as draft (step by step)

Step1 → machine basic
Step2 → pricing
Step3 → location
Step4 → images
Step5 → publish

I can show you the best backend architecture for this wizard system (very commonly asked in senior backend interviews).

If you want, I can explain that next.


You said:
i provide longitude

ChatGPT said:

If you provide longitude (and latitude) from the frontend, then you should store them in GeoJSON format in MongoDB. This allows location-based queries like finding machines near a user.

In MongoDB, the correct format is:

[longitude, latitude]

⚠️ Order is important — longitude comes first.

✅ Recommended Location Schema

Update your schema like this:

TypeScript
location: {
  address: String,
  village: String,
  district: String,
  state: String,
  pincode: String,

  type: {
    type: String,
    enum: ["Point"],
    default: "Point"
  },

  coordinates: {
    type: [Number], // [longitude, latitude]
  }
}

And keep your index:

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ location: "2dsphere" });

📦 Example Stored Data

If your frontend sends:

longitude: 76.348
latitude: 10.015

Then store it like this:

JSON
location: {
  address: "Near bus stand",
  village: "Kakkanad",
  district: "Ernakulam",
  state: "Kerala",
  pincode: "682030",

  type: "Point",
  coordinates: [76.348, 10.015]
}

🧠 Example Backend Controller

When creating a machine:

TypeScript
const { longitude, latitude } = req.body;

const machine = await Machine.create({
  ...req.body,
  location: {
    address: req.body.address,
    village: req.body.village,
    district: req.body.district,
    state: req.body.state,
    pincode: req.body.pincode,
    type: "Point",
    coordinates: [longitude, latitude]
  }
});

🚀 Later You Can Do Location Search

Example: machines within 10 km

TypeScript
Machine.find({
  location: {
    $near: {
      $geometry: {
        type: "Point",
        coordinates: [lng, lat]
      },
      $maxDistance: 10000
    }
  }
});

⭐ Small Professional Tip

Validate coordinates:

longitude → -180 to 180
latitude → -90 to 90

If you want, I can also show you:

  • 📍 How to get latitude & longitude in React / Next.js

  • 📍 How OLX-style “Machines near me” feature works

  • 📍 Best MongoDB query for nearby machines

These will help a lot for your machine rental marketplace backend.


You said:
How to get latitude & longitude in React / Next.js

ChatGPT said:

In React / Next.js, you can get latitude and longitude from the user's browser using the Geolocation API. This is the simplest and most common way.

✅ 1. Basic Example (React / Next.js)

TypeScript
"use client"

import { useState } from "react";

export default function GetLocation() {
  const [location, setLocation] = useState<any>(null);

  const getLocation = () => {
    if (!navigator.geolocation) {
      alert("Geolocation not supported");
      return;
    }

    navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(
      (position) => {
        setLocation({
          latitude: position.coords.latitude,
          longitude: position.coords.longitude
        });
      },
      (error) => {
        console.log(error);
        alert("Location permission denied");
      }
    );
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={getLocation}>Get Location</button>

      {location && (
        <div>
          <p>Latitude: {location.latitude}</p>
          <p>Longitude: {location.longitude}</p>
        </div>
      )}
    </div>
  );
}

📦 Output Example

If user allows location:

Latitude: 10.015
Longitude: 76.348

You can send this to your backend:

TypeScript
coordinates: [longitude, latitude]

✅ 2. Send Location to Backend (Your Machine API)

Example:

TypeScript
const res = await fetch("/api/machines", {
  method: "POST",
  headers: {
    "Content-Type": "application/json"
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    latitude: location.latitude,
    longitude: location.longitude
  })
});

⚠️ Important Notes

1️⃣ Must run on HTTPS

Browser location works only on:

  • HTTPS

  • localhost

2️⃣ User must allow permission

Browser popup:

Allow this site to access your location?

3️⃣ Works only on client side

That is why we use:

TypeScript
"use client"

in Next.js App Router.

🚀 Professional Feature (Better UX)

Instead of button, automatically fetch location:

TypeScript
useEffect(() => {
  navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition((position) => {
    setLocation({
      latitude: position.coords.latitude,
      longitude: position.coords.longitude
    });
  });
}, []);

🧠 Even Better for Your Project

For your machine rental marketplace, best UX is:

1️⃣ User clicks "Use my location"
2️⃣ Auto-fill:

Village
District
State
Pincode

using Reverse Geocoding (Google Maps API).

⭐ Example Flow Used in OLX / Swiggy

User Location → lat/lng
        ↓
Reverse Geocoding
        ↓
Address autofill
        ↓
Save in MongoDB

If you want, I can also show you the complete production-level setup:

  • 📍 Map picker like OLX

  • 📍 Google Maps integration

  • 📍 Auto address detection

  • 📍 Save location in MongoDB

This will make your machine marketplace look like a real startup product.


You said:
import mongoose, { Schema, Document } from "mongoose"; export interface IMachine extends Document { seller: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; machineName: string; category: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; brandModel?: string; yearOfManufacture?: number; condition?: "New" | "Good" | "Used"; machineCapacityHP?: number; fuelType?: "Diesel" | "Petrol"; description?: string; pricing?: { priceType: "Per Hour" | "Per Day" | "Per Acre"; rentalPrice: number; minimumRentalDuration?: number; securityDeposit?: number; }; location?: { address?: string; village?: string; district?: string; state?: string; pincode?: string; type?: "Point"; coordinates?: number[]; }; images?: string[]; operator?: { operatorIncluded: boolean; operatorName?: string; operatorPhone?: string; }; transport?: { transportAvailable: boolean; transportCost?: number; }; isPublished: boolean; wizardStep: number; } const machineSchema = new Schema<IMachine>( { seller: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "User", required: true }, machineName: { type: String, required: true }, category: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "Category", required: true }, brandModel: String, yearOfManufacture: Number, condition: { type: String, enum: ["New", "Good", "Used"] }, machineCapacityHP: Number, fuelType: { type: String, enum: ["Diesel", "Petrol"] }, description: String, pricing: { priceType: { type: String, enum: ["Per Hour", "Per Day", "Per Acre"] }, rentalPrice: Number, minimumRentalDuration: Number, securityDeposit: Number }, location: { address: String, village: String, district: String, state: String, pincode: String, type: { type: String, enum: ["Point"] }, coordinates: { type: [Number], default: undefined } }, images: [String], operator: { operatorIncluded: { type: Boolean, default: false }, operatorName: String, operatorPhone: String }, transport: { transportAvailable: { type: Boolean, default: false }, transportCost: Number }, isPublished: { type: Boolean, default: false }, wizardStep: { type: Number, default: 1 } }, { timestamps: true } ); machineSchema.index({ location: "2dsphere" }); export default mongoose.model<IMachine>("Machine", machineSchema);this is correct

ChatGPT said:

Your schema is almost correct, but there is one important mistake with the GeoSpatial index.

Right now you wrote:

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ location: "2dsphere" });

❌ This is not correct because MongoDB expects the GeoJSON field itself to have the index, not the whole object.

Since your GeoJSON structure is:

TypeScript
location: {
  type: "Point",
  coordinates: [Number]
}

the index should be created on location.coordinates or the whole location GeoJSON object structured correctly.

✅ Correct Way (Recommended)

Modify your schema like this:

TypeScript
location: {
  address: String,
  village: String,
  district: String,
  state: String,
  pincode: String,

  type: {
    type: String,
    enum: ["Point"],
    default: "Point"
  },

  coordinates: {
    type: [Number], // [longitude, latitude]
  }
}

Then add the index:

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ "location.coordinates": "2dsphere" });

📦 Example Stored Document

JSON
{
  "machineName": "Tractor",
  "location": {
    "address": "Near bus stand",
    "village": "Kakkanad",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682030",
    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [76.348, 10.015]
  }
}

Remember:

coordinates: [longitude, latitude]

🚀 Then You Can Do Queries Like

Find machines within 10 km

TypeScript
Machine.find({
  location: {
    $near: {
      $geometry: {
        type: "Point",
        coordinates: [76.348, 10.015]
      },
      $maxDistance: 10000
    }
  }
});

⭐ Small Professional Improvements

1️⃣ Make coordinates default empty

TypeScript
coordinates: {
  type: [Number],
  default: []
}

2️⃣ Add validation

TypeScript
validate: {
  validator: (v: number[]) => v.length === 2,
  message: "Coordinates must be [longitude, latitude]"
}

✅ Final Verdict

Your schema is 95% correct 👍
Only fix:

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ "location.coordinates": "2dsphere" });

instead of

TypeScript
machineSchema.index({ location: "2dsphere" });

If you want, I can also show you a very powerful feature for your project:

🚜 "Find Machines Near Me" API (real production backend)
This is the most important feature for a machine rental marketplace.


You said:
full code

ChatGPT said:

Here is the clean and correct full version of your Machine schema with GeoJSON location support and the correct 2dsphere index.

This version will work properly for "find machines near me" queries.

TypeScript
import mongoose, { Schema, Document } from "mongoose";

export interface IMachine extends Document {
  seller: mongoose.Types.ObjectId;

  machineName: string;
  category: mongoose.Types.ObjectId;

  brandModel?: string;
  yearOfManufacture?: number;

  condition?: "New" | "Good" | "Used";

  machineCapacityHP?: number;

  fuelType?: "Diesel" | "Petrol";

  description?: string;

  pricing?: {
    priceType: "Per Hour" | "Per Day" | "Per Acre";
    rentalPrice: number;
    minimumRentalDuration?: number;
    securityDeposit?: number;
  };

  location?: {
    address?: string;
    village?: string;
    district?: string;
    state?: string;
    pincode?: string;

    type?: "Point";
    coordinates?: number[]; // [longitude, latitude]
  };

  images?: string[];

  operator?: {
    operatorIncluded: boolean;
    operatorName?: string;
    operatorPhone?: string;
  };

  transport?: {
    transportAvailable: boolean;
    transportCost?: number;
  };

  isPublished: boolean;
  wizardStep: number;
}

const machineSchema = new Schema<IMachine>(
  {
    seller: {
      type: Schema.Types.ObjectId,
      ref: "User",
      required: true
    },

    machineName: {
      type: String,
      required: true
    },

    category: {
      type: Schema.Types.ObjectId,
      ref: "Category",
      required: true
    },

    brandModel: String,

    yearOfManufacture: Number,

    condition: {
      type: String,
      enum: ["New", "Good", "Used"]
    },

    machineCapacityHP: Number,

    fuelType: {
      type: String,
      enum: ["Diesel", "Petrol"]
    },

    description: String,

    pricing: {
      priceType: {
        type: String,
        enum: ["Per Hour", "Per Day", "Per Acre"]
      },

      rentalPrice: Number,

      minimumRentalDuration: Number,

      securityDeposit: Number
    },

    location: {
      address: String,
      village: String,
      district: String,
      state: String,
      pincode: String,

      type: {
        type: String,
        enum: ["Point"],
        default: "Point"
      },

      coordinates: {
        type: [Number], // [longitude, latitude]
        default: undefined,
        validate: {
          validator: (v: number[]) => !v || v.length === 2,
          message: "Coordinates must be [longitude, latitude]"
        }
      }
    },

    images: [String],

    operator: {
      operatorIncluded: {
        type: Boolean,
        default: false
      },

      operatorName: String,

      operatorPhone: String
    },

    transport: {
      transportAvailable: {
        type: Boolean,
        default: false
      },

      transportCost: Number
    },

    isPublished: {
      type: Boolean,
      default: false
    },

    wizardStep: {
      type: Number,
      default: 1
    }
  },
  { timestamps: true }
);

// GeoSpatial Index for location search
machineSchema.index({ "location.coordinates": "2dsphere" });

export default mongoose.model<IMachine>("Machine", machineSchema);

✅ Key points in this schema

• coordinates must be stored as

[longitude, latitude]

Example:

JSON
"coordinates": [76.348, 10.015]

✅ Example machine document

JSON
{
  "machineName": "Tractor",
  "category": "664fa8a13d2a",
  "location": {
    "address": "Near bus stand",
    "village": "Kakkanad",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682030",
    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [76.348, 10.015]
  }
}

🚀 With this schema you can build:

• Find machines near user
• Distance based search
• Map based listing (Google Maps / Leaflet)
• Nearby machines sorting

If you want, I can also show you the very important backend API for your project:

GET /machines/nearby

This will return machines within 10km of user location (used by OLX, Uber, Swiggy style apps).


You said:
"use client"; import { useState } from "react"; import { useAuth } from "@/context/AuthContext"; export default function LocationStep({ machineId, nextStep, prevStep }: any) { const { accessToken: ctxToken } = useAuth(); const accessToken = ctxToken || (typeof window !== "undefined" ? localStorage.getItem("accessToken") : null); const [address, setAddress] = useState(""); const [village, setVillage] = useState(""); const [district, setDistrict] = useState(""); const [state, setState] = useState(""); const [pincode, setPincode] = useState(""); const handleSubmit = async () => { if (!address || !district || !state) { alert("Please fill required fields"); return; } try { await fetch( ${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL}/machines/${machineId}, { method: "PUT", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json", Authorization: Bearer ${accessToken} }, body: JSON.stringify({ location: { address, village, district, state, pincode } }) } ); nextStep(); } catch (error) { console.error("Location update failed", error); alert("Failed to save location"); } }; return ( <div className="space-y-4"> <input value={address} placeholder="Address" className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md" onChange={(e) => setAddress(e.target.value)} /> <input value={village} placeholder="Village" className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md" onChange={(e) => setVillage(e.target.value)} /> <input value={district} placeholder="District" className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md" onChange={(e) => setDistrict(e.target.value)} /> <input value={state} placeholder="State" className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md" onChange={(e) => setState(e.target.value)} /> <input value={pincode} placeholder="Pincode" className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md" onChange={(e) => setPincode(e.target.value)} /> <div className="flex gap-3"> <button className="px-4 py-2 border rounded-md" onClick={prevStep} > Back </button> <button className="bg-green-600 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded-md hover:bg-green-700" onClick={handleSubmit} > Next </button> </div> </div> ); }add

ChatGPT said:

You need to add latitude & longitude from the browser and send them to your backend along with the address fields.
Below is the updated LocationStep component with a “Use My Location” button that fetches the coordinates and sends them in the correct MongoDB format.

Key points:

  • Uses Geolocation API

  • Stores [longitude, latitude]

  • Sends type: "Point" and coordinates

Updated LocationStep.tsx

TypeScript
"use client";

import { useState } from "react";
import { useAuth } from "@/context/AuthContext";

export default function LocationStep({ machineId, nextStep, prevStep }: any) {

  const { accessToken: ctxToken } = useAuth();

  const accessToken =
    ctxToken ||
    (typeof window !== "undefined"
      ? localStorage.getItem("accessToken")
      : null);

  const [address, setAddress] = useState("");
  const [village, setVillage] = useState("");
  const [district, setDistrict] = useState("");
  const [state, setState] = useState("");
  const [pincode, setPincode] = useState("");

  const [latitude, setLatitude] = useState<number | null>(null);
  const [longitude, setLongitude] = useState<number | null>(null);

  // Get browser location
  const getCurrentLocation = () => {

    if (!navigator.geolocation) {
      alert("Geolocation not supported");
      return;
    }

    navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(
      (position) => {
        setLatitude(position.coords.latitude);
        setLongitude(position.coords.longitude);
      },
      (error) => {
        console.error(error);
        alert("Location permission denied");
      }
    );
  };

  const handleSubmit = async () => {

    if (!address || !district || !state) {
      alert("Please fill required fields");
      return;
    }

    try {

      await fetch(
        `${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL}/machines/${machineId}`,
        {
          method: "PUT",
          headers: {
            "Content-Type": "application/json",
            Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}`
          },
          body: JSON.stringify({
            location: {
              address,
              village,
              district,
              state,
              pincode,

              type: "Point",
              coordinates:
                latitude && longitude
                  ? [longitude, latitude]
                  : undefined
            }
          })
        }
      );

      nextStep();

    } catch (error) {
      console.error("Location update failed", error);
      alert("Failed to save location");
    }

  };

  return (

    <div className="space-y-4">

      <button
        className="bg-blue-500 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded-md"
        onClick={getCurrentLocation}
      >
        Use My Current Location
      </button>

      {latitude && longitude && (
        <p className="text-sm text-gray-600">
          Lat: {latitude} | Lng: {longitude}
        </p>
      )}

      <input
        value={address}
        placeholder="Address"
        className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md"
        onChange={(e) => setAddress(e.target.value)}
      />

      <input
        value={village}
        placeholder="Village"
        className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md"
        onChange={(e) => setVillage(e.target.value)}
      />

      <input
        value={district}
        placeholder="District"
        className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md"
        onChange={(e) => setDistrict(e.target.value)}
      />

      <input
        value={state}
        placeholder="State"
        className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md"
        onChange={(e) => setState(e.target.value)}
      />

      <input
        value={pincode}
        placeholder="Pincode"
        className="w-full p-2 border rounded-md"
        onChange={(e) => setPincode(e.target.value)}
      />

      <div className="flex gap-3">

        <button
          className="px-4 py-2 border rounded-md"
          onClick={prevStep}
        >
          Back
        </button>

        <button
          className="bg-green-600 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded-md hover:bg-green-700"
          onClick={handleSubmit}
        >
          Next
        </button>

      </div>

    </div>
  );
}

What This Now Does

When the user clicks “Use My Current Location”:

Browser returns something like:

latitude: 10.015
longitude: 76.348

Then your API receives:

JSON
location: {
  address: "...",
  village: "...",
  district: "...",
  state: "...",
  pincode: "...",
  type: "Point",
  coordinates: [76.348, 10.015]
}

This matches your MongoDB GeoJSON schema perfectly.

💡 Next big feature for your project

After this, you can build:

🚜 “Machines Near Me” search

Example API:

GET /machines/nearby?lat=10.015&lng=76.348

Returns machines within 10km.

If you want, I can also show that backend query (very important for your marketplace).

Use returnDocument: 'after' instead. 2026-03-16T10:46:27.693Z [info]: POST /auth/login 200 634.038 ms - 390 2026-03-16T10:46:30.506Z [info]: GET /machines/seller 200 277.228 ms - 4452 2026-03-16T10:46:31.055Z [info]: GET /machines/seller 200 762.581 ms - 4452 2026-03-16T10:46:33.288Z [info]: GET /categories 200 142.171 ms - 1632 2026-03-16T10:46:33.292Z [info]: GET /categories 429 1.386 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:46:45.227Z [info]: POST /auth/refresh 429 0.679 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:46:45.287Z [info]: GET /categories 429 4.168 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:46:45.306Z [info]: GET /categories 429 1.116 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:46:58.718Z [info]: POST /auth/refresh 429 0.638 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:46:58.775Z [info]: GET /categories 429 0.585 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:46:58.791Z [info]: GET /categories 429 0.647 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:47:14.392Z [info]: POST /auth/refresh 429 0.632 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:47:14.490Z [info]: GET /categories 429 1.160 ms - 81 2026-03-16T10:47:14.508Z [info]: GET /categories 429 0.736 ms - 81 (node:13456) [MONGOOSE] Warning: mongoose: the new option for findOneAndUpdate() and findOneAndReplace() is deprecated. { _id: ObjectId('69b7dff9d8fb02604424628d'), seller: ObjectId('69ae9f350ee4c0c0e2ea2f65'), machineName: "nnnn", category: ObjectId('69b23675d866b2b50fc82cc7'), brandModel: "234", yearOfManufacture: 456, condition: "Good", machineCapacityHP: 345, fuelType: "Diesel", description: "bb", location: { type: "Point" }, images: [], operator: { operatorIncluded: false }, transport: { transportAvailable: false }, isPublished: false, wizardStep: 1, createdAt: new Date(1773658105689), updatedAt: new Date(1773658105689), __v: 0 } Point must be an array or object, instead got type missing at InsertOneOperation.handleOk (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongodb@7.0.0\node_modules\mongodb\src\operations\insert.ts:79:13) at tryOperation (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongodb@7.0.0\node_modules\mongodb\src\operations\execute_operation.ts:294:26) at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:105:5) at async executeOperation (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongodb@7.0.0\node_modules\mongodb\src\operations\execute_operation.ts:123:12) at async Collection.insertOne (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongodb@7.0.0\node_modules\mongodb\src\collection.ts:294:12) at async model.$__save (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongoose@9.2.1\node_modules\mongoose\lib\model.js:400:16) at async model.save (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongoose@9.2.1\node_modules\mongoose\lib\model.js:614:5) at async C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongoose@9.2.1\node_modules\mongoose\lib\model.js:2750:9 at async Promise.all (index 0) at async Function.create (C:\AgroShare\node_modules\.pnpm\mongoose@9.2.1\node_modules\mongoose\lib\model.js:2735:11) { errorLabelSet: Set(0) {}, errorResponse: { index: 0, code: 16755, errmsg: Can't extract geo keys: { _id: ObjectId('69b7dff9d8fb02604424628d'), seller: ObjectId('69ae9f350ee4c0c0e2ea2f65'), machineName: "nnnn", category: ObjectId('69b23675d866b2b50fc82cc7'), brandModel: "234", yearOfManufacture: 456, condition: "Good", machineCapacityHP: 345, fuelType: "Diesel", description: "bb", location: { type: "Point" }, images: [], operator: { operatorIncluded: false }, transport: { transportAvailable: false }, isPublished: false, wizardStep: 1, createdAt: new Date(1773658105689), updatedAt: new Date(1773658105689), __v: 0 } Point must be an array or object, instead got type missing }, index: 0, code: 16755 } 2026-03-16T10:48:25.863Z [info]: POST /machines 500 204.675 ms - 648

ChatGPT said:

The error is coming from MongoDB GeoSpatial index validation.
Your document is being saved like this:

JSON
location: { 
  type: "Point"
}

But for a 2dsphere GeoJSON point, MongoDB requires coordinates.

So MongoDB throws:

Point must be an array or object, instead got type missing

because coordinates are missing.

✅ Correct GeoJSON Structure

MongoDB expects:

JSON
location: {
  type: "Point",
  coordinates: [longitude, latitude]
}

Example:

JSON
location: {
  type: "Point",
  coordinates: [76.348, 10.015]
}

🔧 Fix in Backend (Important)

When creating the machine, you must always send coordinates if type: "Point" exists.

Example controller fix:

TypeScript
const { address, village, district, state, pincode, longitude, latitude } = req.body;

const machine = await Machine.create({
  seller: req.user.userId,
  machineName: req.body.machineName,
  category: req.body.category,

  location: longitude && latitude
    ? {
        address,
        village,
        district,
        state,
        pincode,
        type: "Point",
        coordinates: [longitude, latitude]
      }
    : {
        address,
        village,
        district,
        state,
        pincode
      }
});

This ensures coordinates are only added if they exist.

🔧 Fix in Frontend

Send coordinates like this:

TypeScript
body: JSON.stringify({
  location: {
    address,
    village,
    district,
    state,
    pincode,
    type: "Point",
    coordinates: [longitude, latitude]
  }
})

But only if longitude & latitude exist.

Better:

TypeScript
coordinates:
  latitude && longitude
    ? [longitude, latitude]
    : undefined

⚠️ Another Important Warning in Your Logs

You also saw:

mongoose: the `new` option for findOneAndUpdate() is deprecated

Replace this:

TypeScript
findOneAndUpdate(query, update, { new: true })

with:

TypeScript
findOneAndUpdate(query, update, { returnDocument: "after" })

🚀 Best Practice for Your Project

Since you are using multi-step machine creation, location might not exist in Step 1.

So your schema should allow location without coordinates:

TypeScript
coordinates: {
  type: [Number],
  default: undefined
}

Then coordinates can be added in Step 3 (LocationStep).

💡 Your Current Problem in One Line

Your API saved:

location: { type: "Point" }

but MongoDB requires:

location: { type: "Point", coordinates: [lng, lat] }

If you want, I can also show a very clean production structure for your wizard system (step-by-step machine creation).
Right now your backend can be improved a lot for draft machines + step updates.


You said:
76.3843,11.1542 in which place

ChatGPT said:

The coordinates 76.3843, 11.1542 correspond to a location in Kerala, India.

📍 Approximate place:

  • Area: Attappadi

  • District: Palakkad

  • State: Kerala

  • Country: India

It is in the Attappadi tribal region, near the Kerala–Tamil Nadu border, close to the Silent Valley forest area.

📌 Nearby towns:

  • Agali

  • Mannarkkad

  • Anaikatty (towards Tamil Nadu)

🗺️ If you open Google Maps with:

11.1542, 76.3843

you will see the point in Attappadi valley region.

✅ This means if your app saved:

JSON
coordinates: [76.3843, 11.1542]

it correctly represents a location in Attappadi, Palakkad district.

If you want, I can also show how to automatically convert latitude/longitude → village, district, state in your React app (reverse geocoding). This will auto-fill your LocationStep form.


You said:
wht details to get latt,long

ChatGPT said:

To get latitude and longitude, you only need one thing: the user’s device location permission.
The browser then gives you several details.

📍 Data You Get From Browser Location

When you use:

JavaScript
navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition()

you receive a Position object.

Example response:

JSON
{
  "coords": {
    "latitude": 11.1542,
    "longitude": 76.3843,
    "accuracy": 20,
    "altitude": null,
    "altitudeAccuracy": null,
    "heading": null,
    "speed": null
  }
}

✅ Important Fields

FieldMeaning
latitudeNorth/South position
longitudeEast/West position
accuracyAccuracy in meters
altitudeHeight from sea level
headingDirection device is moving
speedSpeed of movement

For your project you only need:

latitude
longitude

🧑‍💻 Simple Code to Get Latitude & Longitude

TypeScript
navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition((position) => {
  const latitude = position.coords.latitude;
  const longitude = position.coords.longitude;

  console.log(latitude, longitude);
});

Example output:

11.1542
76.3843

📦 What You Send to Backend

Your backend expects GeoJSON format:

[longitude, latitude]

Example:

JSON
{
  "type": "Point",
  "coordinates": [76.3843, 11.1542]
}

⚠️ Important order:

longitude first
latitude second

🧠 Where Location Comes From

The browser detects location using:

1️⃣ GPS (mobile devices)
2️⃣ WiFi network
3️⃣ IP address (less accurate)

Accuracy example:

SourceAccuracy
GPS5–10 meters
WiFi20–50 meters
IP1–5 km

🚜 Best Flow for Your Machine Rental App

Seller clicks:

Use my location

Then:

Browser → latitude & longitude
       ↓
Frontend sends coordinates
       ↓
Backend stores in MongoDB
       ↓
Users search nearby machines

✅ Your location schema + frontend is now correct for production use.

If you want, I can also show a very powerful feature used by OLX / Swiggy / Uber:

Auto-fill village, district, state from latitude & longitude (reverse geocoding).
That will make your LocationStep form fill automatically.


You said:
from address

ChatGPT said:

If you want latitude & longitude from an address, you need Geocoding (convert address → coordinates).

There are several APIs, but the most common are:

  • Google Maps Geocoding API

  • OpenStreetMap Nominatim API (free)

  • Mapbox Geocoding API

For learning and small projects, OpenStreetMap Nominatim is easiest.

✅ 1. Get Latitude & Longitude from Address (Free API)

Example address:

Kakkanad, Ernakulam, Kerala

API request:

https://nominatim.openstreetmap.org/search?q=Kakkanad%20Ernakulam%20Kerala&format=json

Response example:

JSON
[
  {
    "lat": "10.0159",
    "lon": "76.3419",
    "display_name": "Kakkanad, Ernakulam, Kerala, India"
  }
]

So:

latitude = 10.0159
longitude = 76.3419

✅ 2. React / Next.js Example

You can convert the form address → coordinates before sending to backend.

TypeScript
const getCoordinates = async () => {

  const fullAddress = `${address}, ${village}, ${district}, ${state}, ${pincode}`;

  const res = await fetch(
    `https://nominatim.openstreetmap.org/search?q=${encodeURIComponent(fullAddress)}&format=json`
  );

  const data = await res.json();

  if (data.length > 0) {
    const lat = parseFloat(data[0].lat);
    const lng = parseFloat(data[0].lon);

    return { lat, lng };
  }

  return null;
};

✅ 3. Send to Backend

TypeScript
const coords = await getCoordinates();

await fetch(`${API_URL}/machines/${machineId}`, {
  method: "PUT",
  headers: {
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}`
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    location: {
      address,
      village,
      district,
      state,
      pincode,
      type: "Point",
      coordinates: coords ? [coords.lng, coords.lat] : undefined
    }
  })
});

📦 Example Stored in MongoDB

JSON
location: {
  address: "Near bus stand",
  village: "Kakkanad",
  district: "Ernakulam",
  state: "Kerala",
  pincode: "682030",
  type: "Point",
  coordinates: [76.3419, 10.0159]
}

🧠 Professional Marketplace Flow

Real apps (OLX, Uber style) do this:

Address typed
     ↓
Geocoding API
     ↓
Latitude + Longitude
     ↓
Save in database
     ↓
Nearby search works

✅ For your machine rental marketplace, best UX is:

1️⃣ User enters address
2️⃣ System automatically converts to coordinates
3️⃣ Store [longitude, latitude] in MongoDB

If you want, I can also show you a very powerful feature used in marketplaces:

Auto-complete address while typing (Google Maps style) — it makes your form look like a real startup product.


You said:
import mongoose, { Schema, Document } from "mongoose"; export interface IMachine extends Document { seller: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; machineName: string; category: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; brandModel?: string; yearOfManufacture?: number; condition?: "New" | "Good" | "Used"; machineCapacityHP?: number; fuelType?: "Diesel" | "Petrol"; description?: string; pricing?: { priceType: "Per Hour" | "Per Day" | "Per Acre"; rentalPrice: number; minimumRentalDuration?: number; securityDeposit?: number; }; location?: { address?: string; village?: string; district?: string; state?: string; pincode?: string; type?: "Point"; coordinates?: number[]; // [longitude, latitude] }; images?: string[]; operator?: { operatorIncluded: boolean; operatorName?: string; operatorPhone?: string; }; transport?: { transportAvailable: boolean; transportCost?: number; }; isPublished: boolean; wizardStep: number; } const machineSchema = new Schema<IMachine>( { seller: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "User", required: true }, machineName: { type: String, required: true }, category: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "Category", required: true }, brandModel: String, yearOfManufacture: Number, condition: { type: String, enum: ["New", "Good", "Used"] }, machineCapacityHP: Number, fuelType: { type: String, enum: ["Diesel", "Petrol"] }, description: String, pricing: { priceType: { type: String, enum: ["Per Hour", "Per Day", "Per Acre"] }, rentalPrice: Number, minimumRentalDuration: Number, securityDeposit: Number }, location: { address: String, village: String, district: String, state: String, pincode: String, type: { type: String, enum: ["Point"] }, coordinates: { type: [Number], // [longitude, latitude] default: undefined, validate: { validator: (v: number[]) => !v || v.length === 2, message: "Coordinates must be [longitude, latitude]" } } }, images: [String], operator: { operatorIncluded: { type: Boolean, default: false }, operatorName: String, operatorPhone: String }, transport: { transportAvailable: { type: Boolean, default: false }, transportCost: Number }, isPublished: { type: Boolean, default: false }, wizardStep: { type: Number, default: 1 } }, { timestamps: true } ); // GeoSpatial Index for location search machineSchema.index({ "location.coordinates": "2dsphere" }); export default mongoose.model<IMachine>("Machine", machineSchema); this basis i want sample real details

ChatGPT said:

Based on your Machine schema, here is a sample real machine document with realistic details (like what a seller would enter in your machine rental marketplace).

📦 Sample Machine Data (Realistic Example)

JSON
{
  "seller": "665f1a2d8c2e4f8d9a1b2345",

  "machineName": "Mahindra 575 DI Tractor",

  "category": "665a12bcde34fa98c1234567",

  "brandModel": "Mahindra 575 DI",

  "yearOfManufacture": 2021,

  "condition": "Good",

  "machineCapacityHP": 45,

  "fuelType": "Diesel",

  "description": "Well maintained tractor suitable for ploughing, rotavator and trailer work. Available for hourly and daily rental.",

  "pricing": {
    "priceType": "Per Hour",
    "rentalPrice": 900,
    "minimumRentalDuration": 2,
    "securityDeposit": 3000
  },

  "location": {
    "address": "Near Govt Higher Secondary School",
    "village": "Kakkanad",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682030",

    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [76.3419, 10.0159]
  },

  "images": [
    "uploads/machines/tractor1.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/tractor2.jpg"
  ],

  "operator": {
    "operatorIncluded": true,
    "operatorName": "Ramesh Kumar",
    "operatorPhone": "9876543210"
  },

  "transport": {
    "transportAvailable": true,
    "transportCost": 1500
  },

  "isPublished": true,

  "wizardStep": 5
}

📍 Location Explanation

JSON
"coordinates": [76.3419, 10.0159]

This represents:

  • Longitude: 76.3419

  • Latitude: 10.0159

📍 Location:

  • Kakkanad

  • Ernakulam District

  • Kerala

  • India

🚜 Another Example (Different Machine)

JSON
{
  "machineName": "Kubota Power Tiller",
  "brandModel": "Kubota RT140",
  "yearOfManufacture": 2022,
  "condition": "New",
  "machineCapacityHP": 14,
  "fuelType": "Diesel",

  "pricing": {
    "priceType": "Per Acre",
    "rentalPrice": 1500,
    "minimumRentalDuration": 1
  },

  "location": {
    "address": "Near Panchayat Office",
    "village": "Kolenchery",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682311",
    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [76.4747, 9.9794]
  },

  "operator": {
    "operatorIncluded": true,
    "operatorName": "Suresh Babu",
    "operatorPhone": "9123456780"
  }
}

🧠 Why Coordinates Matter in Your App

With this schema you can do queries like:

Find machines within 10 km

JavaScript
Machine.find({
  location: {
    $near: {
      $geometry: {
        type: "Point",
        coordinates: [76.34, 10.01]
      },
      $maxDistance: 10000
    }
  }
})

This enables “Machines Near Me” feature.

✅ Your schema structure is good and production-ready for a machine rental marketplace.

If you want, I can also show 3 important improvements for your schema that most senior backend developers add in marketplace apps. These will make your system much more scalable.

🚜 Kubota RT140 Power Tiller (Sample Machine Listing)

Example Data (Your Schema + 4 Images)

JSON
{
  "machineName": "Kubota Power Tiller",
  "brandModel": "Kubota RT140",
  "yearOfManufacture": 2022,
  "condition": "New",
  "machineCapacityHP": 14,
  "fuelType": "Diesel",

  "pricing": {
    "priceType": "Per Acre",
    "rentalPrice": 1500,
    "minimumRentalDuration": 1
  },

  "location": {
    "address": "Near Panchayat Office",
    "village": "Kolenchery",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682311",
    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [76.4747, 9.9794]
  },

  "images": [
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_1.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_2.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_3.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_4.jpg"
  ],

  "operator": {
    "operatorIncluded": true,
    "operatorName": "Suresh Babu",
    "operatorPhone": "9123456780"
  }
}

📍 Location

Coordinates:

[76.4747, 9.9794]

This corresponds approximately to:

  • Village: Kolenchery

  • District: Ernakulam

  • State: Kerala

  • Country: India

💡 Tip for your frontend listing card

Example UI text:

🚜 Kubota RT140 Power Tiller
📍 Kolenchery, Ernakulam
💰 ₹1500 / Acre
👨‍🌾 Operator Included

If you want, I can also generate 10 sample machines with images and realistic Kerala locations so you can seed your MongoDB database for testing your marketplace UI.


You said:
with category name

ChatGPT said:

Here is your same machine data including a category name (since your schema uses a category ObjectId reference to the Category collection).

Example category: Power Tiller

🚜 Kubota RT140 Power Tiller

Example Data with Category

JSON
{
  "machineName": "Kubota Power Tiller",
  "categoryName": "Power Tiller",

  "brandModel": "Kubota RT140",
  "yearOfManufacture": 2022,
  "condition": "New",
  "machineCapacityHP": 14,
  "fuelType": "Diesel",

  "description": "Compact Kubota power tiller suitable for small farms, vegetable cultivation and inter-row farming operations.",

  "pricing": {
    "priceType": "Per Acre",
    "rentalPrice": 1500,
    "minimumRentalDuration": 1
  },

  "location": {
    "address": "Near Panchayat Office",
    "village": "Kolenchery",
    "district": "Ernakulam",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pincode": "682311",
    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [76.4747, 9.9794]
  },

  "images": [
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_1.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_2.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_3.jpg",
    "uploads/machines/kubota_rt140_4.jpg"
  ],

  "operator": {
    "operatorIncluded": true,
    "operatorName": "Suresh Babu",
    "operatorPhone": "9123456780"
  },

  "transport": {
    "transportAvailable": true,
    "transportCost": 1000
  },

  "isPublished": true,
  "wizardStep": 5
}

📂 Category Collection Example

Your Category collection would contain something like:

JSON
{
  "_id": "665a12bcde34fa98c1234567",
  "name": "Power Tiller"
}

Then in your machine document you actually store:

JSON
"category": "665a12bcde34fa98c1234567"

and fetch with:

JavaScript
Machine.find().populate("category")

Result:

JSON
"category": {
  "_id": "665a12bcde34fa98c1234567",
  "name": "Power Tiller"
}

💡 Tip for your UI card

Example display:

🚜 Kubota RT140
📂 Power Tiller
📍 Kolenchery, Ernakulam
💰 ₹1500 / Acre

If you want, I can also generate 10 realistic machine listings with categories (tractor, harvester, rotavator, excavator, power tiller) so you can seed your database and test your marketplace UI quickly.

You said:
each of the description

ChatGPT said:

Below are short, realistic descriptions for each of the 25 machines (5 per category).
These are suitable for your description field in the schema and for frontend display cards.

🌱 Planting & Seeding

1. Kubota Rice Transplanter

Efficient rice planting machine designed for quick and uniform transplanting of paddy seedlings. Ideal for medium to large paddy fields, reducing manual labor and improving productivity.

2. Mahindra Seed Drill

Tractor-mounted seed drill used for accurate seed placement and spacing in fields. Suitable for crops like wheat, maize, and pulses.

3. Vegetable Planter

Specialized planter for sowing vegetable seeds with consistent depth and spacing. Helps improve crop uniformity and reduces planting time.

4. Potato Planter

Mechanized potato planting equipment designed for uniform tuber spacing and depth. Enhances planting efficiency and reduces manual effort.

5. Pneumatic Seeder

Air-powered seeder that ensures precise seed distribution across fields. Ideal for modern farming practices and large agricultural areas.

🌿 Crop Care

1. Fieldking Boom Sprayer

Tractor-mounted boom sprayer designed for uniform spraying of pesticides and fertilizers across large fields. Ensures better crop protection and coverage.

2. Power Sprayer

High-pressure agricultural sprayer suitable for applying pesticides and liquid fertilizers. Compact and efficient for orchards and small farms.

3. Fertilizer Spreader

Equipment used for evenly distributing fertilizers across farmland. Helps maintain proper nutrient balance in soil.

4. Power Weeder

Compact machine used for removing weeds and loosening soil between crop rows. Ideal for vegetable and plantation farming.

5. Orchard Sprayer

Specialized sprayer designed for fruit orchards and plantations. Ensures efficient pesticide spraying for tall trees and dense crops.

🌾 Harvesting

1. CLAAS Combine Harvester

Advanced harvesting machine capable of cutting, threshing, and cleaning crops in one operation. Suitable for large-scale grain harvesting.

2. Paddy Harvester

Machine designed specifically for harvesting rice crops quickly and efficiently in paddy fields.

3. Mini Combine Harvester

Compact harvester suitable for small farms and narrow fields. Provides efficient harvesting with lower fuel consumption.

4. Crop Reaper

Agricultural machine used to cut crops quickly before threshing. Ideal for wheat, rice, and other cereal crops.

5. Sugarcane Harvester

Heavy-duty harvesting equipment designed for cutting and collecting sugarcane efficiently in large plantations.

🚚 Transportation

1. Tractor Trailer

Heavy-duty trailer attached to tractors for transporting crops, fertilizers, and farm materials across farms and markets.

2. Farm Transport Trailer

Multipurpose agricultural trailer designed for carrying harvested crops and farm goods safely.

3. Mini Dump Trailer

Compact dump trailer with hydraulic lifting system for easy unloading of soil, sand, or agricultural produce.

4. Cargo Trailer

Large capacity trailer used for transporting bulk farm goods like grains, vegetables, and fertilizers.

5. Hydraulic Trailer

Advanced trailer equipped with hydraulic tipping system for quick unloading of agricultural materials.

🚜 Land Preparation

1. John Deere 5310 Tractor

Powerful agricultural tractor suitable for ploughing, hauling, and operating various farm implements.

2. Rotavator

Soil preparation implement used for breaking and mixing soil before planting. Ensures better soil aeration and seedbed preparation.

3. Disc Plough

Heavy-duty plough used for deep tillage and turning soil in preparation for planting crops.

4. Cultivator

Farm implement used for loosening soil and removing weeds between crop rows.

5. Power Tiller

Compact tillage machine ideal for small farms and vegetable cultivation. Used for soil preparation and inter-row farming.

✅ These descriptions are short, clean, and professional — perfect for:

  • MongoDB description field

  • Machine detail page

  • Frontend cards

  • SEO

If you want, I can also generate complete JSON seed data for all 25 machines (including name, category, description, pricing, and location) so you can insert them into MongoDB in one command.

You said:
import mongoose, { Schema, Document } from "mongoose"; export interface IMachine extends Document { seller: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; machineName: string; category: mongoose.Types.ObjectId; brandModel?: string; yearOfManufacture?: number; condition?: "New" | "Good" | "Used"; machineCapacityHP?: number; fuelType?: "Diesel" | "Petrol"; description?: string; pricing?: { priceType: "Per Hour" | "Per Day" | "Per Acre"; rentalPrice: number; minimumRentalDuration?: number; securityDeposit?: number; }; location?: { address?: string; village?: string; district?: string; state?: string; pincode?: string; type?: "Point"; coordinates?: number[]; // [longitude, latitude] }; images?: string[]; operator?: { operatorIncluded: boolean; operatorName?: string; operatorPhone?: string; }; transport?: { transportAvailable: boolean; transportCost?: number; }; isPublished: boolean; wizardStep: number; } const machineSchema = new Schema<IMachine>( { seller: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "User", required: true }, machineName: { type: String, required: true }, category: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: "Category", required: true }, brandModel: String, yearOfManufacture: Number, condition: { type: String, enum: ["New", "Good", "Used"] }, machineCapacityHP: Number, fuelType: { type: String, enum: ["Diesel", "Petrol"] }, description: String, pricing: { priceType: { type: String, enum: ["Per Hour", "Per Day", "Per Acre"] }, rentalPrice: Number, minimumRentalDuration: Number, securityDeposit: Number }, location: { address: String, village: String, district: String, state: String, pincode: String, type: { type: String, enum: ["Point"] }, coordinates: { type: [Number], // [longitude, latitude] default: undefined, validate: { validator: (v: number[]) => !v || v.length === 2, message: "Coordinates must be [longitude, latitude]" } } }, images: [String], operator: { operatorIncluded: { type: Boolean, default: false }, operatorName: String, operatorPhone: String }, transport: { transportAvailable: { type: Boolean, default: false }, transportCost: Number }, isPublished: { type: Boolean, default: false }, wizardStep: { type: Number, default: 1 } }, { timestamps: true } ); // GeoSpatial Index for location search machineSchema.index({ "location.coordinates": "2dsphere" }); export default mongoose.model<IMachine>("Machine", machineSchema); above details included full details with image category name not object id
3/3
